title: Drafting Tokens in Parallel
concept: The small draft model generates a sequence of candidate tokens (e.g. 5 tokens) quickly; the large model evaluates all 5 in parallel in a single step.

exercises.prompt: Which model is responsible for quickly generating the initial sequence of candidate tokens?

options.text: The user typing on their keyboard

exercises.explain: A small draft model creates a sequence of candidate tokens very quickly because it is lightweight.

cards.title: Skip the Waiting Line!
cards.body: Why wait for a slow, smart AI to write every single word? What if a faster AI drafted a whole bunch of words ahead, and the smart AI approved them all in one single blink?

cards.title: Drafting in Parallel
cards.body: Instead of checking guesses one by one, the large target model takes a sequence of candidate tokens (like 5 tokens) created by the draft model and evaluates them all at the same time in a single parallel calculation.

cards.title: The Draft and Review Team
cards.body: Think of a fast-writing assistant who drafts a whole paragraph ahead. Even if the chief editor rejects the last few sentences, reviewing them all at once on a single page is much faster than waiting for each sentence to be written one-by-one!

cards.title: A Five-Word Check
cards.body: If the small model drafts the phrase "the cat sat on the", the large model doesn't run 5 separate times. It runs once, checking all 5 words simultaneously to see where the draft got it right.

cards.title: Does it take 5x longer?
cards.body: People think parallel drafting means the large model has to do five times the work. In reality, modern AI hardware is so powerful that evaluating 5 tokens in parallel takes almost the same time as checking just 1!

cards.title: Sequential vs. Parallel Verification
cards.body: See how checking everything at once compares to checking step-by-step.

left.text: Check word 1, then check word 2, then check word 3. Slow!

right.text: Check [word 1, 2, 3, 4, 5] together in a single step. Fast!

exercises.prompt: Why is evaluating a sequence of tokens in parallel so helpful?

options.text: It makes the small model smarter than the large model.
It checks multiple candidate tokens at the same time in one step.
It forces the user to write the text themselves.
It stops the models from using any GPU memory.

exercises.explain: By checking multiple candidate tokens in parallel during a single step, we bypass the need to run the slow model for every individual word.

exercises.prompt: True or False: The large model evaluates all drafted candidate tokens in parallel rather than one-by-one.
exercises.explain: Yes! The large model reviews the sequence of candidate tokens simultaneously in a single forward pass.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe how the large model checks the draft sequence.
exercises.sentence: The target model checks the candidate sequence in [blank] to save time.
exercises.explain: The draft model guesses a sequence of tokens, which the large model evaluates all at once in parallel.

exercises.prompt: Which process describes parallel draft evaluation?

options.text: Draft model writes 1 word -> Large model checks 1 word -> Draft writes next word.
Draft model writes 5 words -> Large model checks all 5 words at the same time.
Large model writes 5 words -> Draft model checks them one-by-one.

exercises.explain: The correct choice shows a fast draft followed by a single parallel verification step. The others show slow step-by-step validation.

exercises.prompt: How does drafting tokens in parallel speed up speculative decoding?

options.text: The draft model generates a sequence of guesses, and the target model checks them all at the same time.
The target model must wait for the draft model to finish the entire book before checking.
The target model checks only the very first token and ignores the rest.
Both models write completely different stories and merge them later.

exercises.explain: The draft model guesses a block of tokens (like 5 at once), and the target model checks them all together in a single forward pass. This is much faster than checking them one-by-one!
